hero.headline: Psychologist for Brazilians and English speakers living abroad
hero.subheadline: Therapy and emotional support for those who live far from home
hero.description: Online sessions available — in-person sessions in Berlin coming soon.
hero.pillar3: Emotional support for migrants and expatriates

intercultural.label: Living Between Cultures
intercultural.title: Living between cultures
intercultural.intro: Living between cultures can be enriching, but also profoundly challenging.
intercultural.p1: The migration process and the difficulties of adapting abroad can trigger emotional responses and emotional instability. It is very common for migrants to begin feeling depressed, anxious, and to develop mental health challenges while living in another country.
intercultural.question1: Do you feel overwhelmed living outside Brazil?
intercultural.question2: Are you experiencing difficulty adapting, loneliness, anxiety, or uncertainty about your future and professional integration?
intercultural.answer: If you answered yes to any of these, I — as a Psychologist and Therapist — can help you find a path to emotional balance and support you through your migration journey abroad.
intercultural.challengesTitle: Many people living outside their home country face:
challenges: Worsening of pre-existing mental health conditions
intercultural.quote: "There is no health without mental health."
intercultural.p2: Mental health is a fundamental part of overall wellbeing. Caring for the emotional dimension is essential for facing life's challenges, especially in contexts of change, adaptation and vulnerability.
intercultural.p3: If you feel this might be an important moment to seek support, therapy can offer a space for listening, understanding and strengthening.
intercultural.ctaNote: Booking a first conversation can be the beginning of this process.

counselling.label: Therapy
counselling.title: Intercultural Psychological Counselling
counselling.description: Migration brings profound changes — to identity, belonging and routine. Living between cultures can be enriching, but also challenging in ways that are often invisible.
counselling.intro: Intercultural counselling offers a safe and qualified space to understand and work through these experiences in all their complexity.
counselling.p1: I work with people going through processes of change and adaptation, including migrants, expatriates and international students, as well as women re-entering the workforce, mothers living outside their home country, and people facing health, stigma, or significant life transitions such as separations and new beginnings.
counselling.p2: Psychological support can also play an important preventive role, helping to recognise early signs of emotional overload, anxiety or exhaustion before they become more intense.
counselling.p3: Throughout the process, we work on strengthening internal resources, expanding emotional awareness and building more sustainable ways of dealing with challenges — whether in the personal, professional or relational sphere.

individual.title: Individual psychological counselling
individual.p1: Living between cultures can be a rich experience, but also profoundly challenging. Often, what is lost is not only the physical space we once occupied in our country, but our history, our social and professional connections, our emotional and family ties — and the specific experiences of those who migrate from the southern to the northern hemisphere, where we lose the tropical climate, the sun, and other aspects of our culture.
individual.p2: In this limbo of intercultural adaptation, we lose our internal reference points: the sense of belonging, identity and continuity. Adapting to a new country can bring loneliness, anxiety, emotional overload, and a feeling that is hard to explain — of not belonging, 'neither here nor there'. Has it ever happened to you that you went back to your home country and missed the host country? (or vice versa).
individual.p3: Individual psychological support offers a safe and welcoming space for you to talk about all of this, at your own pace, in your own language, from your own story.
individual.p4: Through an intercultural approach, the therapeutic process allows you to understand and integrate migration experiences, working through losses, transitions and internal conflicts that are part of this journey — enabling you to develop personal strategies and resources to support your integration in the host country and your psychosocial wellbeing, which is crucial in preventing the worsening of your emotional challenges.
individual.p5: Rather than diagnosing mental pathologies or symptoms, the aim is to investigate the triggers causing these emotional difficulties, develop resilience and coping mechanisms, and recover emotional balance and health. From this perspective, the goal is to develop autonomous and functional individuals in their migration journey, strengthen internal resources and rebuild a sense of self, even in contexts of change and uncertainty.
individual.closing: You do not need to go through this process alone.

group.title: Group counselling
group.p1: Group sessions offer a structured space for sharing, reflection and collective processing.
group.p2: Through an intercultural approach, group work integrates therapeutic elements with the strength of shared experience. The exchange between people facing similar challenges fosters identification, belonging and new forms of understanding. At the same time, the group is facilitated in a careful, professional and empathic way, ensuring a safe, ethical and welcoming environment where each participant can express themselves at their own pace, whenever they wish. Knowledge is built through this exchange, and collective listening becomes a tool for transformation and motivation in situations that can feel paralysing for the individual.
group.p3: This combination of clinical listening and peer support allows participants not only to better understand their own experiences, but also to expand their internal resources and strengthen their ability to deal with everyday challenges.

approach.title: The intercultural psychology approach
approach.p1: Intercultural psychology is a field of psychology dedicated to understanding how cultural factors influence the way we think, feel and relate to the world.
approach.p2: In the context of migration, this approach recognises that emotional experiences cannot be separated from culture, language, life history and the social contexts in which each person exists. Living between cultures involves more than practical adaptation — it entails transformations in identity, in the sense of belonging, and in the way a person perceives themselves and is perceived by others.
approach.p3: This approach considers these dimensions in an integrated way, offering a listening space that takes into account not only the individual, but also their history, culture and life context.
approach.p4: From this perspective, psychological support is not limited to symptoms, but seeks to understand suffering in its full complexity, taking into account processes such as displacement, loss of reference points, rebuilding of bonds and negotiation between different cultural systems.
approach.p5: At the same time, this approach allows us to recognise both the challenges and the resources that emerge from the intercultural experience, supporting the integration of these experiences and the construction of new ways of being in the world — with greater internal coherence and emotional grounding.

techniques.title: Other therapeutic techniques used
items: Narrative Exposure Therapy (NET) / Forensic Narrative Exposure Therapy (FORNET)

challenges.title: Main challenges addressed in counselling
items: Integration into the job market and life in Germany
Strategies for interacting with government institutions (Child Protection Services, Employment Agency, Job Centre, Local Authority)
Racism and xenophobia (in school, workplace and daily life)
Parenting and supporting children with digital burnout
Lack of access to public mental health services
Support for people with substance dependency

about.label: About Me
about.title: About me
about.desc1: I am a Psychologist trained at Universidade São Marcos in São Paulo (CRP: 06/55288) with a postgraduate specialisation in Psychology and Migration from PUC Minas. I have over 20 years of experience in therapeutic intervention and psychological counselling with people in situations of psychosocial vulnerability and psychological distress.
about.desc2: In Brazil, I worked for nearly 9 years as a volunteer for GIV – Grupo de Incentivo à Vida, providing psychotherapy and preventive counselling for antiretroviral treatment, primarily for the LGBTQI+ community, women, children and adolescents living with HIV-Aids. I also held a private psychotherapy practice in São Paulo.
about.quote: "For 11 years, my migration journey in Berlin has become an instrument of my work — combined with my solid academic and therapeutic training."

t1.title: Psychologist — Brazilian Embassy in Berlin
t1.desc: Since February 2025, I have been working as Psychologist at the Brazilian Embassy in Berlin, providing psychological and psychosocial counselling to Brazilians in vulnerable situations. This service is free of charge to the Brazilian community and is based on a brief intercultural intervention approach. Alongside this, I see Brazilian migrants and English-speaking clients online.

t2.title: Intercultural Psychologist — Berlin
t2.desc: In 2014, I migrated to Berlin for family reasons, and it became my permanent home. Experiencing motherhood for the first time as a migrant woman, I deepened my studies in psychology and migration, finding in this approach practical tools for psychological and psychosocial support that make a real difference to the quality of life of Brazilians living in Europe.

t3.title: Psychologist — German Red Cross · ZIK
t3.desc: Psychological support for migrants, refugees and people in vulnerable situations in Berlin.

t4.title: Psychosocial Coordinator — MSF · ARK · Africa
t4.desc: In 2007, I began my migration journey working with Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) in Mozambique, where I coordinated a mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) department in humanitarian projects focused on HIV-Aids, drug-resistant Tuberculosis and natural disasters. I also worked in Eswatini and with organisations such as ARK and the Government of Mozambique, in cooperation with the UN, World Bank, UNICEF and PEPFAR.

t5.title: Clinical Psychologist — Brazil
t5.desc: Nearly 9 years as a volunteer at GIV – Grupo de Incentivo à Vida, providing psychotherapy and preventive counselling for the LGBTQI+ community, women, children and adolescents living with HIV-Aids, alongside private clinical practice in São Paulo.

thirdSector.label: Third Sector
thirdSector.title: Mental Health and Psychosocial Support (MHPSS)
thirdSector.description: I offer consulting for organisations, institutions and projects working in multicultural, humanitarian and complex contexts, with a focus on developing and strengthening mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) strategies.
thirdSector.p1: My work combines clinical experience, programme coordination and the development of policies and guidelines across different international contexts.
thirdSector.p2: Throughout my career with Médecins Sans Frontières and other organisations, I have worked on HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and community health projects in African countries including Mozambique and Eswatini, developing and coordinating MHPSS programmes in contexts of high vulnerability. This experience also includes protocol development, team training, technical supervision and implementation of innovative community-centred care models.
thirdSector.p3: In collaboration with the Ministry of Health of Mozambique, I developed national psychosocial support guidelines in the context of HIV/AIDS, which were subsequently integrated into the country's treatment programme.
areas: Development and implementation of Mental Health and Psychosocial Support (MHPSS) programmes, including needs assessment, strategy design and intervention monitoring
Consulting on HIV/AIDS and public health contexts, with a focus on treatment adherence, stigma reduction and strengthening psychosocial care
Training, supervision and capacity building of multidisciplinary teams in mental health, psychosocial support and intercultural work
Supporting organisations in developing preventive mental health strategies and wellbeing in institutional and corporate environments
thirdSector.audience: Non-governmental organisations (NGOs), international agencies, humanitarian projects, public and private institutions working with populations in vulnerable situations or in multicultural contexts.
thirdSector.contactDesc: Development and evaluation of projects in non-governmental and humanitarian organisations, mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) programmes and interventions. Also consulting for private companies implementing preventive mental health programmes for employees with a migration background. For projects and partnerships, get in touch to start a conversation and develop a tailored proposal.

corporate.label: Workplace Mental Health
corporate.title: Corporate environments and international teams
corporate.description: I develop and facilitate workshops and training programmes aimed at promoting mental health and psychological wellbeing in institutional and corporate environments.
corporate.p1: These interventions help prevent psychological illness at work, supporting organisations in reducing stress, strengthening individual and collective resources, and promoting healthier and more sustainable workplaces.
corporate.p2: Through an intercultural approach, content is tailored to the specific needs of each organisation, particularly in contexts marked by cultural diversity and the presence of international teams.
topics: Mindfulness and mindful awareness practices in professional life
Psychological resilience in contexts of change
Communication and interpersonal relationships at work
Mental health in migration and cultural adaptation
corporate.methodology: The training programmes combine psychoeducational elements, individual reflection and group dynamics, creating safe spaces for learning and exchange. In addition to group activities, it is possible to integrate individual sessions for employees who need psychological support, broadening the care and effectiveness of the interventions. I also offer psychosocial support for the integration of international professionals and their families, focusing on cultural adaptation, reduction of stressors and strengthening wellbeing through the migration process.
impact: Reduction in absenteeism related to stress and psychological illness
Decreased risk factors associated with burnout
Improved organisational climate and working relationships
Strengthened retention of professionals, especially in international teams
Support for diversity and inclusion strategies for international professionals in multicultural contexts
corporate.format: Workshops, training sessions and learning programmes can be delivered online or in person, in Portuguese or English, for small teams or large groups.

contact.label: Contact
contact.title: Let's talk
contact.description: If you are going through a challenging time, seeking support can be an important first step. I am available for individual sessions, group sessions and institutional consulting, online or in person in Berlin. Get in touch to ask questions or schedule a first conversation.

faq.label: FAQ
faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Answers to the most common questions before getting started.

items.question: How does the first session work?
items.answer: The first session is a space for listening and welcoming. We talk about what brought you here, your expectations, and how I can support you. It is also an opportunity for you to get to know my approach and decide whether you would like to continue.

items.question: What platform is used for online sessions?
items.answer: Sessions take place via video call on secure platforms such as Google Meet or Zoom. You will receive the access link before each session.

items.question: How long are the sessions?
items.answer: Individual sessions last 50 minutes. Group sessions last 2 hours and take place fortnightly.

items.question: Is confidentiality guaranteed in online sessions?
items.answer: Yes. Professional confidentiality is a fundamental ethical principle of psychology and applies fully to online sessions, in accordance with the professional code of ethics.

items.question: Do you accept health insurance?
items.answer: At the moment, sessions are self-funded and not covered by health insurance. For questions about fees and payment options, please get in touch.

items.question: How often should I attend therapy?
items.answer: The ideal frequency varies depending on each person's situation. In general, weekly or fortnightly sessions support the continuity and depth of the therapeutic process. We will discuss together what makes most sense for you.
